What happened on May 3rd, 2018 won't soon be forgotten by any of the participants who were there. First on the scene is Officer Jared Carlson of the St. George Police Department. Duly noting Jessica's commentary on his police work, the officer nonetheless goes on to provide her with a full reading of her Miranda rights. It must be said that Jessica does raise an interesting point, based on a US Supreme Court decision. It was held that the term interrogation under Miranda refers not only to express questioning, but also to any words or actions on the part of the police that the police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response from the suspect. However, just because police ask questions doesn't necessarily implicate Miranda rights. It's the nature of those questions that has to be considered. In this case, Jessica was asked at least one question that was likely designed to elicit an incriminating response, and therefore, she should have been Mirandized prior.

Jessica may be abrasive, but she's not wrong to question her arrest, and this was touched on previously. An officer yanking a suspect out of their home could be deemed a violation of the Fourth Amendment. There are limited circumstances, however, in which police have the right to arrest a suspect in this way, including the pursuit of a fleeing criminal. And whether it's an after the fact justification or not, Officer Carlson is correct when he says he'd ordered her to stay put before she fled into the house. I didn't do. I didn't do a goddamn thing. I didn't do a goddamn thing. You know it, man. I'm coming. I'm going to kill you all, man. Trust me. Trust me. Like y'all did me one too many times, bro. Come open my door now. I'll see you in three years, dog. I'll see you in three years.

Though this was the last of Willie Mosher's threats on camera, it was apparently not the last he would deliver. In his police report, Officer Lusk said that he visited Willie and Jessica in the Washington County Jail the next day. He brought with him a vicious animal letter that recommended that Champ the German Shepherd be put to sleep. According to Lusk, Willie stated that he would kill anyone involved if his dog was put down. Fortunately, things didn't come to that. In a statement on May 25th, 2018, St. George Mayor John Pike announced that after obtaining reviews from several independent animal behavior experts, the city had decided to give Champ a second chance. Of course, according to Willie, there was much more to the story of how Champ was spared than the pardon cited in the statement.

They said they brought in three experts and they deemed Champ vicious. They put Champ in a box in a kennel, never even hardly let him out. He just sat back there scared out of his mind. They deemed him to be euthanized. People started kind of hearing the story. The officer actually tried to say that he bit him and then and he changed his story and said, no, he didn't bite me. But I guess the video went viral. There was a bunch of people that were like, no, this isn't right. That dog didn't do nothing. He doesn't deserve to die. And the city they tried to just push it through and kill him. Well, a lawyer out of Bountiful, Utah, saw the whole story, heard about it and said, no, no, no, no. And he actually by the grace of God, he said, I'll take his case. But I didn't even know this guy. He stopped the killing at 9:30 that morning. He was supposed to be killed at 11.

According to Willie, additional experts evaluated Champ and found that he wasn't vicious. He was just scared. During the course of the interview, Willie also shed some light on the relationship he and Champ shared. It seems they were fated to be together.

Champ was Champ was everything. I don't have kids and so Champ, I got Champ when he was actually six weeks old. I spent every day with Champ. We ran in the hills every day and he was everything. He everyone knew me, knew Champ. He went everywhere I went. Everybody knew him. He would do anything to protect me.
Sadly, Champ the German Shepherd died in the summer of 2023.

He died in August. I went away for two days that I went down to watch some of my players play in the Little League World Series. The people that were watching him said that he was just moping around. I said, take him to the hills then because that was his spot. We would run for for hours. They said that he went up to jump in to get into my truck and he put his paws up on my truck and then he just laid down. They called me about 10 minutes later and they said, no, he's not breathing very good, Willie. I said, take him to the vet right now. And they went to pick him up to take him to the vet and he died right there.
Willie couldn't believe Champ was gone. He tells how he went to see his loyal friend one last time to say goodbye.

I was just hysterical. It was hard for me to really believe he was dead. I laid down with him and he blinked his eye at me. And I said, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa. And then all of a sudden his tail moved. And all of a sudden he lifted his paw a little bit. And I said, call the vet. I said, what the hell is this? I'm convinced now that he he waited for me to get there. And I laid there with him for about two hours and I I walked out and I came back and there was no movement. But when I got home, there was something his soul was inside that body. He moved and he blinked at me. And I said, I'm not I'm not crazy. And then I went buried him.

After Willie's account of his last moments with Champ, he was asked if there was a final message he felt was essential to share. He expressed his concern that perhaps other dogs could falsely be deemed aggressive and suffer a much worse fate than Champ did. After all, if his attorney hadn't intervened, Champ likely wouldn't have gotten a second chance.

I think they would kill any dog any moment and I don't think they would even take anything into consideration like, hey, the dog was scared and you did this and this is I think these people will kill a dog. They tried to kill Champ and Champ never even bit him and never ever even had another problem before that. So I'm like, what's the criteria for a dog to be killed? Just if he scares somebody? And that's what was so heinous about it was they were trying to kill Champ and he never touched him.

Willie and Jessica themselves had most of their serious charges dismissed, though Jessica pleaded guilty to interfering with an arresting officer, for which she was sentenced to probation and community service. Willie pleaded guilty to the same charge, as well as a charge of intoxication.
